why: States an internal feeling as fact rather than describing observable behaviour.
suggestion: Describe what was seen and heard, e.g. raised voice, clenched fists, what happened just before.

why: Names a feeling without evidence; 'appeared upset when...' tied to observation is fine, a bare label is not.
suggestion: Record the observable signs (crying, withdrew, went quiet) and the trigger.

why: Asserts an internal state as fact.
suggestion: Describe behaviour: tearful, head down, quiet; note what preceded it.

why: A bare positive label can mask issues and gives no evidence.
suggestion: Describe what the child did or said that suggested this (smiling, laughing, sought out activity).

why: Labels mood as fact and carries judgement.
suggestion: Describe the specific changes observed and when they occurred.

why: Judgemental shorthand for unspecified behaviour.
suggestion: State what was observed (short replies, frowning, declined breakfast) and any trigger.

why: Asserts an internal state and implies a pattern without evidence.
suggestion: Record the observable behaviour, time and context instead of the mood label.

why: A bare clinical-sounding label without observed signs or trigger.
suggestion: Use qualified, evidenced wording: 'appeared anxious when... (pacing, asking repeated questions)'.

why: Names a state as fact without evidence.
suggestion: Describe the signs observed and what was happening at the time.

why: Interprets feeling as fact; fine only when tied to observation ('expressed frustration by...').
suggestion: Describe how frustration was expressed and the trigger.

why: Attributes a feeling and implied motive without evidence.
suggestion: Record what the child said or did; quote their own words if they named a feeling.

why: Interpretation of internal state presented as fact.
suggestion: Describe behaviour (left activity, said 'this is boring' in quotes, looked away).

why: Judgement-laden feeling label with no evidence.
suggestion: Describe observable presentation and context.

why: Informal feeling label stated as fact.
suggestion: Quote the child's words or describe the behaviour that suggested this.

why: Intense feeling label asserted as fact.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviour, volume, words used and what preceded them.

why: Asserts feeling as fact.
suggestion: Describe what was seen/heard and the trigger.

why: Interprets internal state without evidence.
suggestion: Record the observable response and what it followed.

why: Vague informal label for arousal level with no description.
suggestion: Describe the actual activity level (running between rooms, talking quickly) and duration.

why: Judgemental interpretation of withdrawal.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour neutrally (sat apart, did not respond when spoken to) and the context.

why: Vague catch-all label that records nothing specific.
suggestion: Name what was observed (tearful, shaking, raised voice) and when.

why: Judges the child's character rather than describing behaviour.
suggestion: Record the specific task, what the child did or did not do, and any context (tiredness, understanding).

why: A character judgement that hides what was actually said or done.
suggestion: Quote or describe the words/actions and the situation in which they occurred.

why: Moral judgement of the child rather than a record of behaviour.
suggestion: Describe the specific action, time and what happened before and after.

why: Judgemental label that doesn't record what was said.
suggestion: Quote the child's words and describe the tone and context.

why: Blames the child's character and family without evidence.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and the circumstances; avoid commentary on upbringing.

why: Attributes calculated intent to a child; stigmatising and often misreads attachment-seeking behaviour.
suggestion: Describe what the child said/did to whom, in sequence, without interpreting intent.

why: Imputes deceitful character.
suggestion: Record the observed actions factually and let the sequence speak for itself.

why: Character judgement implying dishonesty.
suggestion: Describe the action seen (e.g. took item from kitchen at 7pm) without trait labels.

why: Labels the child rather than recording the discrepancy observed.
suggestion: Record what the child said and what was found, and note the difference factually.

why: Behaviour-as-identity; destroys trust in the record and the child.
suggestion: State what was said and what evidence differed, e.g. 'X said... ; staff observed...'.

why: Moral judgement of character.
suggestion: Describe the specific interaction and what each person did.

why: Trait label instead of a description of choices made.
suggestion: Record what was offered/asked, the child's response, and any reason they gave.

why: Developmental judgement made informally; belongs in assessment by qualified professionals, not labels.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviour and the child's age/stage context factually.

why: Dismissive judgement of a child for being a child.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour without comparison or judgement.

why: Summarises with judgement and records no actual events.
suggestion: List the specific behaviours, times and triggers instead of a verdict.

why: Judges attitude rather than recording words/actions.
suggestion: Quote what was said and describe tone, audience and context.

why: Moralises about the child's response; irrelevant and judgemental in a record.
suggestion: Record what was offered and how the child responded, factually.

why: Frames needs-expression as a character flaw.
suggestion: Record what the child asked for, how often, and the responses given.

why: Dismissive label for attachment-seeking behaviour.
suggestion: Describe proximity-seeking behaviour neutrally (stayed close to carer, asked carer not to leave).

why: Pathologises a child's need for care.
suggestion: Describe what the child sought and how adults responded.

why: Locates the problem in the child as a trait.
suggestion: Describe the specific situation, behaviour and what made it hard to manage.

why: Judges the child as obstructive instead of recording the interaction.
suggestion: Record the request, the response, and any reasons the child gave.

why: Hostile character judgement with no place in a professional record.
suggestion: Quote/describe exactly what was said or done and to whom.

why: Imputes malicious character and motive.
suggestion: Describe the action and sequence of events without inferring intent.

why: Imputes scheming character to a child.
suggestion: Record observed actions in time order without interpretation.

why: Adultifies and criminalises a child's behaviour.
suggestion: Describe what happened step by step; leave analysis to assessment.

why: Emotive judgement that records nothing usable.
suggestion: Describe the words/actions, who was present and what preceded them.

why: Judgemental summary instead of description.
suggestion: Record what was said/done and the other child's response.

why: Strongly emotive judgement revealing the writer's feelings, not the facts.
suggestion: Quote the language used (or summarise factually) and describe the context.

why: Turns behaviour into identity and is too vague to evidence risk or need.
suggestion: Describe the specific actions (shouted, kicked door, raised fist), target, duration and trigger.

why: Labels the child oppositional instead of recording the interaction.
suggestion: Record the instruction given, the child's response and any reasons offered.

why: Defines the child by the adult's experience of them.
suggestion: List the actual events of the shift/visit with times and context.

why: As a bare label it gives no detail needed for risk assessment.
suggestion: Describe exactly what physical actions occurred, to whom, with what outcome and trigger.

why: Vague school-style label that records no observable event.
suggestion: State what the child did, in what setting, and the effect observed.

why: Locates the challenge in the child rather than the situation; describes nothing.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviour and what was happening around the child.

why: Informal whole-child label defined by adult burden.
suggestion: Record the specific behaviours and supports needed instead.

why: Identity label that follows a child through their file and biases readers.
suggestion: Record each incident factually and separately.

why: Defines the child as the problem.
suggestion: Describe the behaviours, needs and contexts; the child is not the problem.

why: Mocking, gendered identity label that dismisses distress.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and intensity neutrally with the trigger.

why: Identity label that dismisses need; attention-seeking is often attachment-need.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and consider recording it as seeking connection/support.

why: Identity label; record bullying behaviour, not a bully identity.
suggestion: Describe the specific incident, those involved and actions taken.

why: Quasi-diagnostic label used informally without assessment.
suggestion: Describe activity level, duration and context; leave diagnosis to clinicians.

why: Demeaning, gendered identity label.
suggestion: Describe the words/behaviour and context without characterising the child.

why: Identity label defining the child as frightening or bad.
suggestion: Record specific behaviours with times and triggers.

why: Labels the child unstable without describing what changes and when.
suggestion: Describe the observed changes in presentation, how quickly, and around what events.

why: Dehumanising language that should never appear in a child's record.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour factually and respectfully.

why: Vague animalising label.
suggestion: Describe what the child actually did, where, and for how long.

why: Frames the child as beyond help and the adult as blameless.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour, the strategies tried and the outcome of each.

why: Defines a child by harm done to them; stigmatising and hopeless.
suggestion: Describe experiences and current needs/behaviours specifically and respectfully.

why: Vague identity label that records nothing actionable.
suggestion: Name the specific worries, behaviours or events being referred to.

why: As a bare label of a child it is vague and stigmatising.
suggestion: Quote or summarise the actual words/actions, target and context.

why: Claims to know the child's intent, which cannot be observed.
suggestion: Describe the action and sequence; record intent only if the child stated it (in quotes).

why: Asserts intent as fact.
suggestion: Record what was observed; note 'X said he meant to...' only if said.

why: Intent is interpretation, not observation.
suggestion: Describe the action and context without inferring purpose.

why: Imputes motive and dismisses possible unmet need.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and adult responses; consider what need it may communicate.

variants: knew exactly what he was doing
why: Claims access to the child's mind and awareness.
suggestion: Record observable actions; let assessment consider understanding.

why: Imputes motive and centres the adult's feelings.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and your professional response, not the imputed aim.

why: Imputes manipulative intent in slang form.
suggestion: Record what the child requested or did and the response given.

why: Attributes deliberate provocation.
suggestion: Describe the interaction in sequence without inferring intent.

why: Interprets motive; often used as a catch-all instead of description.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviour and the boundary/rule involved.

phrase: playing one off against the other
variants: played us off against each other
why: Imputes strategy and manipulation.
suggestion: Record what was said to each adult and when; let the pattern be evidenced.

why: Asserts motive that cannot be observed.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour, audience and responses without inferring purpose.

why: Imputes hostile motive.
suggestion: Record what happened and any words the child used.

variants: has to have his own way
why: Reduces the child's wishes to wilfulness.
suggestion: Record what the child asked for and how the disagreement unfolded.

why: Dismisses expressed distress or injury as exaggerated for gain.
suggestion: Record the reported pain/distress and observations neutrally.

variants: put it on for the social worker
why: Accuses the child of faking distress; dangerous in safeguarding records.
suggestion: Record what the child reported and what was observed, without judging authenticity.

why: Dismisses possible genuine distress or injury.
suggestion: Record the child's report and observable signs; seek medical advice if relevant.

why: Mocks and dismisses a child's crying as performance.
suggestion: Record that the child cried, when, and the context.

why: Imputes intent to offend before anything has happened.
suggestion: Describe the observed behaviour and setting.

why: Imputes opportunistic motive in slang.
suggestion: Record the request or action and the response factually.

why: Dismisses a child's account; in safeguarding this can suppress disclosures.
suggestion: Record exactly what the child said in their words, and separately any conflicting information.

why: Declares intervention futile and forecloses the child's future.
suggestion: Record what was tried, the outcome, and what might be tried next.

why: Writes the child off with a prediction stated as fact.
suggestion: Record current behaviour and any changes over time, positive or negative.

why: Fixed prediction that biases future readers against the child.
suggestion: Record the repeated behaviour factually with dates so patterns are evidenced, not asserted.

why: Abandons professional hope and purpose; harmful in any record.
suggestion: Record needs, strategies tried and outcomes; escalate if current support isn't working.

variants: a leopard never changes its spots
why: Cliché asserting permanent character.
suggestion: Stick to dated, factual records of behaviour over time.

why: Attributes behaviour to immutable nature.
suggestion: Describe behaviour and context; avoid claims about nature.

why: Asserts innate, unchangeable cause.
suggestion: Record observed behaviour and relevant history factually.

why: Stigmatises the child and family with hereditary judgement.
suggestion: Record the child's own behaviour; family history belongs in assessment, stated factually.

why: Closes down problem-solving with an absolute claim.
suggestion: List the strategies tried, when, and the specific outcomes of each.

why: Records professional abandonment of a child.
suggestion: Record the difficulty and escalate for support/supervision rather than recording resignation.

why: Dismisses a child or a whole day as worthless.
suggestion: Record what actually happened, including anything that went well.

variants: that's just the way she is
why: Whole-child summary that normalises and stops curiosity about causes.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviour and look for triggers/patterns instead of accepting it as fixed.

why: Records nothing and assumes the reader knows the 'usual'.
suggestion: Briefly describe what actually happened, even if it matches previous days.

why: Asserts a pattern as character rather than evidencing it.
suggestion: Record the event factually; patterns should emerge from dated records.

why: Sweeping claim of constancy without evidence.
suggestion: Describe today's events; reference specific previous dates if relevant.

why: Frames behaviour as confirmation of a fixed character.
suggestion: Record the event on its own terms with context.

why: Whole-child summary that explains nothing.
suggestion: Describe the actual behaviour observed.

why: Cynical commentary implying the child's behaviour is inevitable.
suggestion: Remove commentary; record events and context only.

why: Dismissive shorthand that records nothing.
suggestion: State what happened today, with times and triggers.

why: Dismisses events as repetitive instead of recording them.
suggestion: Record the day's events briefly; repetition is itself useful evidence when dated.

why: Assumes a known pattern instead of evidencing it.
suggestion: Describe what occurred and cross-reference earlier dated entries if needed.

why: Sweeping constancy claim.
suggestion: Describe today's observations specifically.

why: Frames the child as a foregone conclusion.
suggestion: Record the event without editorial framing.

why: Slang that hides what actually happened and its seriousness.
suggestion: Describe the specific behaviours (shouting, throwing, hitting), trigger, duration and how it was resolved.

why: Dismissive slang that judges instead of describing.
suggestion: State the specific behaviours, when and where they occurred.

why: Vague slang carrying judgement.
suggestion: Record the observable behaviour and the situation around it.

why: Jargon-slang that interprets behaviour as performance of distress without describing it.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour itself and its context.

why: Judges behaviour as performance for an audience.
suggestion: Describe what the child did and who was present.

why: Mocking slang for distress.
suggestion: Describe the distress shown, the trigger and how the child was supported.

why: Dismissive label that hides intensity, duration and trigger.
suggestion: Describe what the child did, for how long, what preceded it and what helped.

why: Belittling slang for distress or anger.
suggestion: Record the observable behaviour and context neutrally.

why: Dismissive (and ethnically loaded) slang for a child's distress.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour, trigger and support given.

why: Catch-all label that obscures what happened; particularly unhelpful for autistic children where detail matters.
suggestion: Describe the behaviours, sensory/situational triggers, duration and recovery.

why: Dismisses the child's response as excessive.
suggestion: Record what the child said/did and what they were responding to.

why: Records the adult's irritation, not the child's behaviour.
suggestion: Remove personal commentary; describe behaviour and professional responses.

why: Centres adult frustration rather than the child's needs.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and supports; raise adult stress in supervision, not the record.

why: Slang that trivialises distress or anger.
suggestion: Describe what was observed and the trigger.

why: Slang implying irrationality without describing events.
suggestion: Describe the escalation step by step with the trigger.

why: Slang that dismisses and records nothing usable.
suggestion: Record the specific behaviour, audience, duration and resolution.

why: Stigmatising mental-health slang applied to a child's behaviour.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour factually; never use mental-health terms as insults.

why: Hyperbolic slang that hides the actual events.
suggestion: Describe the behaviours, intensity and duration specifically.

why: Slang substituting for description of escalation.
suggestion: Record the sequence: trigger, behaviour, response, outcome.

why: Hyperbolic idiom rather than description.
suggestion: Record that the child screamed/shouted, for how long, and the context.

why: Vague dismissive idiom.
suggestion: State the actual behaviour observed.

why: Vague slang that judges activity as worthless.
suggestion: Describe what the child was doing and the setting.

why: Judgemental label for unspecified behaviour.
suggestion: Describe the actions; 'silly' tells a future reader nothing.

why: Dismissive slang for slowness or indecision.
suggestion: Describe what the child was doing and how long the task took.

why: Judges pace as a fault.
suggestion: Record times factually (e.g. took 20 minutes to get ready).

why: Regional slang mocking low mood or sulkiness.
suggestion: Describe the presentation and context neutrally.

why: Slang judgement of irritability.
suggestion: Describe tone, words and trigger instead.

why: Dismisses a child's complaints as noise.
suggestion: Record what the child was complaining about and how it was addressed.

why: Dismissive judgement of the child's communication.
suggestion: Record the content of what the child said and the response.

why: Dismisses complaints rather than recording them.
suggestion: Note what the child raised, in their words if possible, and any action taken.

why: Dismissive slang for crying or complaining.
suggestion: Record that the child cried/complained, the apparent trigger and the support given.

why: Regional slang dismissing repeated requests as pestering.
suggestion: Record what the child asked for, how often, and the responses.

why: Compliance-praise idiom that records nothing observable and equates 'good' with quiet.
suggestion: Describe what the child actually did (activities, interactions, mood indicators).

why: Sentimental label with no observational content.
suggestion: Record specific positive behaviours and interactions.

variants: butter wouldn't melt in his mouth
why: Implies the child is deceptively innocent; a disguised character judgement.
suggestion: Record observed behaviour without insinuation.

why: Dismisses expressed distress as performance.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour and intensity factually with the trigger.

why: Euphemism that records nothing and implies a known pattern.
suggestion: Describe what happened, the duration and the support given.

why: Idiom rather than observation; vague for monitoring purposes.
suggestion: Describe the activity level and what the child did.

why: Judges the child's response as disproportionate without describing it.
suggestion: Describe the response and the trigger; let readers judge proportion from facts.

why: Hyperbole instead of observation.
suggestion: Describe the actual activity (running, jumping on furniture), duration and setting.

why: Sweeping claim that hides events and implies hopelessness.
suggestion: List the specific behaviours, the strategies used and their effect.

why: Hyperbole with no measurable content.
suggestion: Describe intensity concretely (volume, duration, actions).

why: Figurative intensity claim with no detail.
suggestion: State what increased, by how much, compared with when.

why: Summarises with drama instead of facts.
suggestion: List the events in order with times.

why: Violent hyperbole inappropriate in a child's record.
suggestion: Describe what actually happened and any damage or injury factually.

why: Dramatic summary that records nothing specific.
suggestion: Record the actual events, participants and outcomes.

why: Flippant hyperbole that trivialises conflict in the record.
suggestion: Describe the disagreement, who was involved and how it was resolved.

why: Dramatic idiom in place of a factual account.
suggestion: Record the sequence of events with times.

why: Vague and dramatic; the detail matters for risk and repair.
suggestion: List what was damaged or displaced, the trigger and how the child was supported after.

why: Hyperbole that obscures the actual scale of damage.
suggestion: Itemise damage factually with photographs/repair notes where relevant.

why: Hyperbole instead of observation.
suggestion: Record that the child screamed, for roughly how long, and the context.

why: Unlikely-literal intensity claim.
suggestion: Estimate frequency or duration honestly (e.g. repeatedly between 4pm and 6pm).

why: Conveys the adult's exhaustion rather than the child's behaviour.
suggestion: Record frequency and duration factually.

why: Hyperbolic frequency claim.
suggestion: State when and how often the behaviour was actually observed.

why: Intensity judgement with no description.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour itself so its seriousness is evidenced.

why: Hyperbole that criminalises group behaviour.
suggestion: Describe what each child did and how staff responded.

why: Frames the child's choice as defiance and omits how the request was made.
suggestion: Record what was asked, how, and the child's response/words (e.g. 'X said no when asked to...').

why: Frames the interaction around adult control rather than the child's perspective.
suggestion: Describe what was asked, the child's response, and any reasons or worries they expressed.

why: Clinical compliance jargon that treats a child's autonomy as a fault.
suggestion: State what was requested and what the child chose to do, with their stated reasons.

why: Blames the child/family for a service relationship problem.
suggestion: Record what was offered, when, how, and the response; consider barriers to engagement.

why: Frames the child as an enforcement problem.
suggestion: State the expectation, what happened, and the child's perspective if known.

why: Obedience framing judges the child rather than recording the interaction.
suggestion: Record the instruction, the response, and the context.

why: Conflates not complying with not hearing; vague and judgemental.
suggestion: Record what was said to the child and what the child did or said in response.

phrase: wouldn't do as he was told
variants: wouldn't do as she was told
why: Obedience framing that omits the request and context.
suggestion: State the request, the child's response and any negotiation.

why: Labels the child as a barrier rather than describing actions.
suggestion: Describe specifically what the child did and the effect.

why: Vague control-framed term; in physical contexts the detail is safeguarding-critical.
suggestion: Describe exactly what happened, including any physical contact, who did what and why.

why: Asserts internal stance rather than recording the response.
suggestion: Record what was offered and what the child said or did.

why: Praises compliance as the goal; can mask fear or masking in looked-after children.
suggestion: Describe what the child did and their apparent comfort, not their obedience.

why: Compliance language obscures whether the child was genuinely okay or shut down.
suggestion: Describe behaviour and presentation; note quietness or withdrawal as observations.

why: Frames the record around obedience.
suggestion: Describe the activity and the child's participation instead.

why: Slang compliance framing.
suggestion: State what was asked and what the child did.

why: Absolute claims are rarely accurate and bias future readers.
suggestion: Use dated examples and honest frequency (e.g. on three occasions this week).

why: Absolute negative that forecloses exceptions and hope.
suggestion: State observed frequency with dates rather than 'never'.

why: Sweeping pattern claim without evidence.
suggestion: Cite the actual occasions with dates.

why: Exaggerated frequency presented as fact.
suggestion: Record realistic frequency and timing.

why: Often inaccurate absolute; patterns need evidence.
suggestion: Log occurrences with dates so the pattern is verifiable.

why: Absolute hopeless claim.
suggestion: List what has been tried and the specific outcomes.

why: Absolute claim of impossibility.
suggestion: Record who has tried what and the results.

why: Unattributed consensus presented as evidence.
suggestion: Name who said what, when, and in what context.

why: Absolute regularity claim.
suggestion: Give honest, dated frequency.

why: Absolute plus interpretation; 'ignored' assumes the child heard and chose not to respond.
suggestion: Record 'did not respond when asked/spoken to' with the specific instances.

why: Blanket reassurance that records no evidence and can mask concerns.
suggestion: Describe what the child did, said and how they presented.

why: 'Fine' is an opinion, not an observation; serious case reviews repeatedly criticise it.
suggestion: Replace with specific observations: activities, interactions, mood indicators, anything notable.

why: Pre-judges significance and may close down later scrutiny.
suggestion: Record the facts and let analysis sit separately; note why a worry was discounted, with evidence.

why: A conclusion without the observations that support it.
suggestion: Briefly record what was checked/observed that supports the conclusion.

why: Summarises absence rather than recording presence of anything.
suggestion: Note key observations of the visit/shift even when uneventful.

why: Empty entry that wastes a recording opportunity and can hide drift.
suggestion: Record brief positives and routine observations (meals, school, contact, mood).

why: Opinion summary with no observable content.
suggestion: Say what made it good: activities, achievements, interactions.

why: Judgement summary that hides the actual events.
suggestion: Record what happened, when, with triggers and responses.

why: Records nothing; routine detail still has monitoring value.
suggestion: Add brief factual notes on routine, mood and interactions.

why: Care-home shorthand that means little without behavioural detail.
suggestion: Describe what the child did and how they presented during the period.

why: Equates quiet compliance with wellbeing and records no detail.
suggestion: Describe participation, interactions and mood rather than a behaviour verdict.

why: Casual blanket reassurance with no evidence.
suggestion: Add the observations that support the judgement.

why: Assumes the reader shares the writer's expectations.
suggestion: State briefly what actually happened.

why: Positive but evidence-free summary.
suggestion: Cite the specifics: attendance, achievements, relationships, self-care.

why: Hides drift; 'no change' in a poor situation is itself significant and needs evidencing.
suggestion: State what was observed this time and compare explicitly with dated previous entries.

why: Presents the writer's inference as self-evident fact.
suggestion: State the observable signs that led to the impression, and frame it as impression.

why: Asserts interpretation as undeniable without evidence.
suggestion: Replace with the evidence: what was seen/heard that supports the view.

why: Converts inference into certainty.
suggestion: Use 'appeared' or 'seemed' tied to the observed signs.

why: Claims the interpretation needs no evidence.
suggestion: Provide the observation and qualify the inference.

why: Eliminates appropriate professional uncertainty.
suggestion: State the evidence and the level of confidence honestly.

why: Certainty about interpretation rather than observation.
suggestion: Reserve certainty for directly observed facts; qualify interpretations.

why: Speculation dressed as deduction.
suggestion: Record what is known; flag unknowns as unknowns.

why: Asserts the child's knowledge or awareness without evidence.
suggestion: Record what the child said and did; assessment can consider understanding.

why: Signals speculation presented as insight.
suggestion: State the facts and clearly label any hypothesis as professional opinion.

why: Frames inference about internal state as direct perception.
suggestion: Describe what was visible and use qualified language ('appeared', 'seemed').

why: Behaviour always has a function; this records the adult's failure to identify the trigger as a fact about the child.
suggestion: Describe what was happening in the 5–10 minutes before; if no trigger was identified, say 'no trigger was identified'.

why: Erases antecedents that matter for understanding and planning.
suggestion: Record the preceding events, setting and interactions, even if the link is unclear.

why: Asserts absence of trigger as fact and frames the child as randomly dangerous.
suggestion: Describe the lead-up factually; note 'staff did not observe a trigger' if true.

why: May reflect missed early signs rather than their absence.
suggestion: Describe the moments before; note any early signs in hindsight.

why: Dismisses behaviour as meaningless.
suggestion: Record the context and timing; patterns may emerge across entries.

why: Claims certainty that no trigger existed rather than that none was observed.
suggestion: Write 'no trigger was identified/observed' and describe the surrounding context.

why: Often substitutes for describing the build-up.
suggestion: Check whether earlier signs occurred and record the sequence.

why: An instruction/opinion, not a record; moralises about the child.
suggestion: Record what happened; put goals in the plan, framed around support not correction.

why: Retrospective judgement rather than a record of events.
suggestion: Record what happened; reflective judgements belong in supervision or analysis sections.

why: Moral judgement that ignores development, trauma and context.
suggestion: Record the behaviour and the child's age/understanding factually.

why: Prescription embedded in a record.
suggestion: Note the observed need factually and raise the goal in planning.

why: Punitive prescription that doesn't belong in observational recording.
suggestion: Record events and responses; behaviour-support planning is a separate, considered process.

why: Dismissive moral instruction aimed at a child.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour; remove commentary.

why: Judges effort, which cannot be observed, and prescribes.
suggestion: Record what the child did and any barriers observed.

why: Opinion/instruction rather than record.
suggestion: Keep recommendations in the plan or analysis, clearly labelled.

why: Prescriptive and assumes a deficit of understanding.
suggestion: Record what was explained, the child's response, and any apparent understanding.

why: Punitive satisfaction has no place in a child's record.
suggestion: Remove entirely; record events and responses neutrally.

why: Judges the child against an undefined norm; stigmatising.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour specifically and note any change from the child's own baseline.

why: Pejorative normalcy judgement.
suggestion: Describe exactly what the child did or said.

why: Judges rather than describes; obscures potentially significant detail.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour precisely; unusual presentations need detail, not adjectives.

why: Vague normalcy judgement.
suggestion: Describe what was unusual compared with the child's baseline, factually.

why: Judges deviation without describing it.
suggestion: State the specific observation and what differed from usual presentation.

why: Stigmatising mental-health slang.
suggestion: Describe behaviour factually; never use 'crazy' about a child.

why: Slang that pathologises distress without describing it.
suggestion: Describe the distress shown, the trigger and the support given.

why: Grossly stigmatising; clinical terms must never be used as insults or informally.
suggestion: Describe the behaviour; if a mental-health concern exists, record observations and seek clinical input.

why: Repeated impersonal reference erases the child's identity from their own record.
suggestion: Use the child's name (or initials per local policy) throughout.

why: Bureaucratic shorthand that depersonalises records children may later read.
suggestion: Use the young person's name; avoid 'YP' abbreviations.

why: Treats the child as a case object.
suggestion: Name the child; reserve formal designations for legal documents where required.

why: Legalistic depersonalisation in everyday recording.
suggestion: Use the child's name.

why: Refers to a child or their home as 'the placement', reducing them to a service arrangement.
suggestion: Name the child and describe the home/carers; write as if the child will read it one day.

why: System-centred jargon that depersonalises.
suggestion: Use the person's name.

why: Process-centred recording that says nothing about the child's experience of family time.
suggestion: Describe how the child responded before, during and after family time.
